author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: Dr. Smith, I trained my brother on an inexpensive older generation 3D printer. He's had his about 4 years. The improvements that Elegoo has made in this area are phenomenal. So much so, when he asked about updating his printer, I made him purchase an Elegoo as his replacement. It was half the price of his old unit, and he will get better results. I absolutely love my Elegoo Saturn. I still have my old filament printer, because I know what to expect from it. Besides, I've switched to resin.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i agree mate - they are becoming something of a flagship company in this area

author: @mouse_mc
text: Can I run the 3D printer off a ups? I rarely have like 9hrs of constant grid power

replies.author: @Dave_D.
replies.text: Depends on how big the battery is.  I have the Plus, and I measured the power usage -  when everything is running (hotend heater, bed heater) it uses 450w.  Once heated, it fluctuates between 20w and 70w as it just runs the motors and pulses the heaters to maintain temp.  a 3.5 hr print used .5 kwh of electricity.  So they don't use that much, really....and the Plus is one of the bigger ones.

replies.author: @mouse_mc
replies.text: @Dave_D. thx, still getting my head around kWh and Ah of batteries. Let's say we don't have to worry about start, that we do with grid power on, but need a ups battery to cover a 2 hr power interruption. Would a 100Ah cover say 50w requirement. Or should I say, would for instance one of those 12v deep cycle batteries they use on gate motors work. Does the printers have a 12V converter plug like laptop or would I need an inverter. Thank appreciate the help, really considering getting one, but I know this is one of my obstacles. We currently get 2 hr interruptions 3 times per day, sometimes goes up to 4 hrs at a time.

replies.author: @brandonstrong-bl6ss
replies.text: ​@mouse_mc I'm in the same boat with the same questions

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes you can

replies.author: @Dave_D.
replies.text: @mouse_mc 100ah @ 12v is 1200wh.  Depending on the type of battery would determine how much of that you should use (ie  let's say lead acid is not recommended to go below 50%).  So if we say you have 600wh of capacity, and the printer averages 50w/h (once heated up and running), should let you run it for 12 hrs.  Even allowing for start up, I figured I could run it 7 hrs per kwh, so that's 4 hrs run time.  You would also need an inverter, which would give some loss on top of that.  Consider this info w/ a grain of salt...it's late, I just got home from work, and I'm tired...so I may have some #'s wrong.  LOL
